description: Edge case test - special characters in node labels (parentheses, brackets, colons, slashes, ampersands)

expected_attack_paths.rationale: Main attack path through web application tier to database via load balancer
Cache server may contain sensitive session data or cached database queries
S3 storage may contain uploaded files, backups, or sensitive data
Direct web server compromise leading to database access

ransomware.rationale: No backup visible, database and storage are targets. Cache and load balancer provide minimal protection.

application_vulns.rationale: Load balancer present (good), but no WAF or firewall. Web server directly exposed.

phishing.rationale: No MFA visible. If users access web UI, compromised credentials = full access.

insider_threat.rationale: No access logging, no segmentation. Direct paths from web tier to all backend services.

dos.rationale: Load balancer provides some DoS protection. Cache helps with availability. But no DDoS mitigation.

supply_chain.rationale: Multiple dependencies (MySQL, Redis, S3, CloudFront) but reasonable architecture. Version tracking visible (Web Server v2.1.3).

parser_validation_notes.purpose: Test parser's ability to handle special characters in labels
parser_validation_notes.expected_parser_behavior: Extract node IDs (Node1-Node6) correctly, preserve full labels with special chars, parse edges with protocol labels

rationale: Basic architecture with minimal security controls, designed to test parser robustness with special characters. Load balancer and cache provide some protection, but missing fundamental controls like WAF, firewall, network segmentation. Medium risk due to direct paths from entry points to sensitive data stores.
